title: The box task - a method for assessing in-vehicle system demand
author: Trommler, Morgenstern, et al.

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: The setup of the method follows the differentiation of the Dimensional Model of Driver Demand [11] into physical and cognitive demand. Thus, physical demand is assessed by the BT and cognitive demand by the DRT. The setup is shown in Fig. 1.Fig. 1Setup of the Box Task and DRT. In addition to the widely used DRT, participants interact with a steering wheel and gas pedal to perform the Box Task.Fig 1

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: The DRT is an ISO-standardized test procedure [4]. Within the present method, a tactile DRT is used. A vibration module placed on participants‘ shoulders gives a tactile signal (vibration) in a random interval of three to five seconds. Participants have to react to this signal by pressing a button at the steering wheel. To get an indication about cognitive demand, hit rate and mean reaction time are calculated (for more details see [4]).

steps.title: Method details
steps.instruction: The BT simulates a car-following scenario. Within the BT, a displayed box changes its lateral position (~ lane maintenance) and size (~ headway to a lead vehicle) continuously in a sinusoidal pattern. Participants have to adjust to these changes by moving the steering wheel (lateral position of the box) and the gas pedal (size of the box). That means, participants are instructed to keep the box within an inner and outer boundary (see Fig. 2). To get an indication about visual-manual demand, error states are calculated.Fig. 2Box Task (BT), example screen. The blue box's position and size change continuously. The two yellow squares represent the guide boxes (i.e., inner and outer boundaries).Fig 2

steps.title: Experimental procedure
steps.instruction: To assess the distraction potential of secondary tasks/in-vehicle systems with the BT+DRT, an experimental study has to be conducted. For our studies (see [7,8]), we used a laboratory setting. Hence, the participants sat in front of a computer/monitor with a gas pedal and a steering wheel. They were informed that the Box Task represents the primary task, i.e., they should “drive” as safely as possible while simultaneously engaging in different secondary tasks. The primary and secondary tasks were explained by written instructions. Before each test block, participants could practice the BT+DRT until they felt comfortable. Afterwards, participants had to perform a baseline run (i.e., BT+DRT without secondary task engagement) and the dual-task conditions.

steps.title: Experimental procedure
steps.instruction: Participants had to use the gas pedal und steering wheel to keep the box within the boundaries. Within each trial, participants had 30 seconds to get familiar with the BT. After 30 seconds, the dual-task conditions started. An exclamation mark and a beep sound were presented as a prompt to start with secondary task engagement. Additionally, participants had to respond to the haptic signal of the DRT. The experimental trials ended after 210 seconds.

steps.title: Experimental procedure
steps.instruction: This procedure was repeated for all dual-task conditions. For more details, see Morgenstern, Wögerbauer et al. [7] and Morgenstern, Trommler et al. [8].

steps.title: Data analysis
steps.instruction: Participants’ BT performance can be divided into lateral and longitudinal errors. Longitudinal errors occur when the dynamic box leaves the boundaries, i.e., the box becomes either smaller than the inner boundary or larger than the outer boundary (see Fig. 3, left). A lateral error exists if the size of the dynamic box is within the boundaries, but exceeds the outer boundary either on the left or on the right side (see Fig. 3, right). In addition to the number and/or duration of lateral and longitudinal error states, the variability of the box size and the box position is assessed. For this, the standard deviation from the ideal box size and position is used.Fig. 3Examples of longitudinal (left) and lateral (right) error states.Fig 3

steps.title: Data analysis
steps.instruction: For each trial, a logging file is created. Next to a header (i.e., time of experiment and participants’ ID), the following data are stored for each frame:-Angle wheel (i.e., lateral change of the steering wheel)-Angle pedal (i.e., deflection of the gas pedal)-Position of the box in pixels-Size of the box in pixels

steps.title: Data analysis
steps.instruction: After data preparation, different measures can be calculated (e.g., with Matlab):-Number of lateral and longitudinal error states-Duration of lateral and longitudinal error states-Mean lateral position (i.e., tendency to hold the box more to the left or more to the right)-Mean longitudinal size (i.e., tendency to hold the box smaller or larger compared to the ideal box position)-Standard deviation of lateral position-Standard deviation of longitudinal size

steps.title: Data analysis
steps.instruction: Participants’ DRT performance can be determined by calculating the hit rate and the reaction time for correct answers (see [4]).
